title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
description:
Implement `js_divergence(p, q)` which computes the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence** between two discrete distributions:

```
M = (P + Q) / 2
JSD(P || Q) = (KL(P || M) + KL(Q || M)) / 2
```

JSD is always in `[0, log(2)]` and is symmetric: `JSD(P||Q) = JSD(Q||P)`.

The square root `sqrt(JSD)` is a proper distance metric.

**Why this matters:** JSD fixes KL's asymmetry and infinity issues. It is used as a loss in GANs (the original GAN paper minimizes JSD between real and generated distributions) and in comparing language model outputs.
constraints: Must be symmetric: js_divergence(p, q) == js_divergence(q, p)
p and q must be valid probability distributions
starter_code:
import numpy as np

def kl_divergence(p, q):
    """KL(P||Q) for discrete distributions (provided for reference)."""
    p = np.asarray(p, dtype=float)
    q = np.asarray(q, dtype=float)
    if np.any((p > 0) & (q == 0)):
        return np.inf
    mask = p > 0
    return float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log(p[mask] / q[mask])))


def js_divergence(p, q):
    """Compute Jensen-Shannon Divergence between P and Q.

    Args:
        p: 1-D array-like — probability distribution P
        q: 1-D array-like — probability distribution Q

    Returns:
        Scalar JSD in [0, log(2)]
    """
    pass
explanation:
**Key concepts:**

1. **Symmetry:** Unlike KL, JSD is symmetric. JSD(P||Q) = JSD(Q||P) because M = (P+Q)/2 is the same mixture regardless of order.

2. **Bounded:** JSD is always in [0, log(2)] (with natural log). This is because both KL(P||M) and KL(Q||M) are bounded by log(2) — M is never less than half of either P or Q.

3. **No infinity:** Even if P and Q have disjoint support, JSD remains finite (unlike KL which would be infinite).

4. **GAN connection:** The original GAN objective (Goodfellow 2014) is equivalent to minimizing JSD between the real data distribution and the generator distribution. This is one reason GANs can fail — JSD saturates when distributions have disjoint support.

5. **Metric:** `sqrt(JSD)` satisfies the triangle inequality, making it a proper metric on probability distributions.

test_cases.name: Identical distributions give 0
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
p = [0.3, 0.4, 0.3]
assert abs(js_divergence(p, p)) < 1e-9

test_cases.name: Symmetric
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
p = [0.1, 0.4, 0.5]
q = [0.3, 0.3, 0.4]
assert abs(js_divergence(p, q) - js_divergence(q, p)) < 1e-9

test_cases.name: Bounded by log(2)
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
p = [1.0, 0.0]
q = [0.0, 1.0]
jsd = js_divergence(p, q)
assert jsd <= np.log(2) + 1e-9
assert abs(jsd - np.log(2)) < 1e-9

test_cases.name: Non-negative
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
p = [0.2, 0.5, 0.3]
q = [0.4, 0.1, 0.5]
assert js_divergence(p, q) >= 0

test_cases.name: Partial overlap
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
p = [0.5, 0.5, 0.0]
q = [0.0, 0.5, 0.5]
jsd = js_divergence(p, q)
assert 0 < jsd < np.log(2)

hints: M = (p + q) / 2 is always a valid distribution if p and q are
JSD = (KL(P||M) + KL(Q||M)) / 2 — M has support wherever either P or Q has support, so no infinities
